messages.content: What do you think of the vision?
What do you think of the spec and especially of Attention Language?
what is dmiensionality of chatgpt embedding space?

OpenAI’s public embedding models have fixed output sizes, but “ChatGPT embedding space” isn’t one single official space unless you mean a specific embedding model. For example, `text-embedding-3-large` supports up to 3072 dimensions and `text-embedding-3-small` up to 1536, while older `text-embedding-ada-002` is 1536-dimensional.

If you mean the hidden internal representation space of a ChatGPT model during generation, OpenAI does not publicly specify it as a single embedding-space dimension in the way API embedding models are specified. If you want, I can also explain the difference between token embeddings, hidden state width, and API embedding vectors.
You have AttentionLanguage in your context, correct?
Yes. I have the AttentionLanguage spec you provided in context for this conversation, including its current sigils and wording.
Can you see what Sigil I'm on?
Yes. You’re currently on Libs > AttentionLanguage > Contrast.
What do you think of what I wrote?

I think it’s strong and alive. “Contrast” feels like one of the load-bearing concepts in your ontology, and the move from sensory axes to aggregated evaluative axes like good vs bad gives it real explanatory reach.

The strongest part is that you’re naming something phenomenologically native instead of importing “dimension” from ML just because it is standard. The weak spot is that the entry currently mixes at least three things: perceptual axes like brightness, evaluative axes like good vs bad, and directional preference axes like less vs more, so I’d want sharper boundaries between those if you want the term to stay crisp.

Thank you for giving me the terminology to distinguish the three kinds of contrasts, but I think perceptual and evaluative are the same thing for me. Contrasts weave together from primitive to complex. So a lot of simple things like a combination of temperature readings and brightness and pain are bad for me. And other combinations are good. I strive for good, I avoid bad. So, from my point of view, it's the same. Same with what I called categorical and you called directional contrasts. I want more or less of this quantity.

Yes, that makes sense, and I think your ontology gets more coherent if you lean into that instead of separating them artificially. In your language, a contrast is not just a neutral measurement axis but a lived axis of difference that can compose into higher-order felt meaning, including good and bad.

So I’d refine my reaction this way: the important distinction may not be between perceptual and evaluative, but between primitive contrasts and aggregated contrasts, where aggregation preserves continuity with lived experience. And directional contrast then reads not as a separate type but as a polarity structure inside contrast itself, where preference gives the axis its orientation.
Alright, so could you tell me what the distinction between primitive and these weaved or compound aggregated contrasts are? I do have an example of it.

I’d put the distinction in terms of how directly a contrast is given versus how much interpretation is required to stabilize it. A primitive contrast is one you can treat as directly read from experience at your working scale, while an aggregated contrast is one whose position is computed, implicitly or explicitly, from many other contrasts across time, context, or embodiment.

So brightness can feel primitive because you experience variation along it immediately, while good vs bad is aggregated because it is a settled result of many underlying contrasts being woven together into one usable orientation. The key thing is that this is not an ontological absolute but a modeling choice relative to the observer, scale, and resolution.

I don't have any direct readouts. My senses don't deliver to me readouts about photons or individual sound packets. Yet a whole frame, I don't know where it came from, and it's been aggregated over some window.

Yes, exactly. So “primitive” should not mean physically fundamental or preprocessed by nothing, because from your point of view everything already arrives collapsed into experience.

In your language, a primitive contrast is just one that is available as an immediate usable distinction within a frame at your current scale and resolution. An aggregated contrast is then a contrast woven from other contrasts within your model, not necessarily by the body before experience, but by the observer or agent as it compresses frames into orientation like good, danger, safety, intimacy, or progress.
I think all these distinctions are actually without a difference. It's always a contrast. The only serious distinction is between directional and the polar one.

That feels cleaner and more faithful to what you’re actually seeing. Then “contrast” stays as the single general unit, and the meaningful internal split is about topology of the axis: polar contrasts span between two named opposites, while directional contrasts express orientation toward more or less without requiring two qualitatively different poles.

Even there, I’d stay a little suspicious, because “dark ↔ bright” can also be read as less ↔ more brightness. So the real distinction may be linguistic rather than structural: some contrasts are naturally named by opposing qualities, others by monotonic change in one quantity.

Well, okay, let me tell you how I see it. So I see, since I'm inhabiting my sigils, I only see other sigils as intersections with mine. So they appear as things that I recognize and name and about which I have preferences, but the preference is always the same contrast: less or more.

That’s very strong. It suggests that “less vs more” is not just one contrast among others but the form preference takes from within an inhabited sigil, while named things are the contents preference attaches to.

So maybe other oppositions like dark vs bright are observational contrasts, but from the agent’s point of view all actionable contrast collapses into directional preference: more of this, less of that. That gives you a clean split between recognizing what is there and orienting toward or away from it.

Is there a better word to describe it? I've been struggling with contrasts. I initially used them because they were about these unions of opposites. But then there are real unions or tensions between the presence and absence of a quality.

Yes. “Contrast” is good for felt difference, but it gets strained once you want one word to cover oppositional qualities, magnitude, absence-presence, and preference-direction all at once.

The closest stronger candidates I see are “axis,” “gradient,” and “polarity.” “Axis” is the cleanest general word for a variable along which things can differ, “gradient” is better if lived orientation and movement matter, and “polarity” is best if the tension between opposed tendencies is central.

From what you said, I suspect “axis” for the general case and “polarity” for the special case may fit you better than “contrast.” If you want, I can help rewrite the Contrast sigil using each of those words so you can feel which one your language wants.
But isn't an axis like contrast? Or is it neutral, because I can't just call it with one word? Brightness Axis

Yes, “axis” is more neutral. “Contrast” already implies felt difference or opposition, while “axis” just says there is some ordered degree of variation along which observations can be located.

So “brightness” can name the axis by itself, and “brightness axis” is just the more explicit form when needed. That may actually help your language, because then the one-word name is the quality itself, and you don’t have to force every axis to sound like a pair of opposites.
Are you able to rename a word in the code, I mean in the text? do you have a rename tool?
Yes, I have a rename tool that can rename a sigil and update all `@references` to it across the whole sigil tree. If you want, tell me which sigil to rename and what new name you want.
I tried to rename Contrast to Axis. It renamed the folder, but it didn't rename any of the references.

Yeah, I can see how that happened. Your references are mixed-case and inconsistent, like `@disposition`, `@dispositions`, and `@Disposition`, so a strict sigil-rename likely only caught exact forms or failed after the folder had already changed.

I can fix the text, but there isn’t a bulk plain-text find-and-replace tool here, only sigil-level operations and full rewrites of context language. If you want, I can inspect the affected contexts and rewrite the relevant language files so the references consistently use `@Disposition` or `@axes` where appropriate.
Now it found nothing. The tool is supposed to take care of all of these variations and normalize folder names, I mean the Sigil names, to capitalized and then respect the case and plurals and -ing.

That sounds like the tool’s rename logic is using the current filesystem state as source of truth, so once the folder already became `Axis`, trying to rename `Contrast` again has no source to match and returns nothing. In other words, the structural rename happened, but the text migration phase likely either failed or was skipped, and now the tool can’t infer the old name from the renamed sigil alone.

If you want, I can inspect the current `Axis` context and nearby contexts, then manually rewrite the affected language files to restore consistency. After that, you could decide whether to keep `Axis` or switch again to a better word.
